name: Babs Gonzales
abstract: Babs Gonzales (October 27, 1919 – January 23, 1980), born Lee Brown, was an American bebop vocalist, poet, and self-published author. His books portrayed the jazz world that many black musicians struggled in, portraying disk jockeys, club owners, liquor, drugs, and racism.

about_quotes.speaker: Sonny Rollins
about_quotes.evidence: he gave me an opportunity... I got a chance to work with a lot of great people... I got a chance to play with all these guys
about_quotes.doc_title: Sonny Rollins — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: James Moody
about_quotes.evidence: Babs [phonetic sp.] was with me singing... I got a phone -- what I did was I did standards. 'Cause Eddie Jefferson came with me, you know. Babs [phonetic sp.] was with me singing
about_quotes.doc_title: James Moody — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mary Lou Williams
about_quotes.evidence: He used to live down the street from me... I used to meet him... I was trying to baby him... the nicest guy that I've ever seen in my life.

about_quotes.speaker: Jo Jones
about_quotes.evidence: I came up and took him to Doctor Hawkins in my arms. I took him in my hand, in my own arms and took him across the street

about_quotes.speaker: Jones Hank
about_quotes.evidence: If we were working in Nice... Suddenly, Babs Gonzales would show up and do his thing. You couldn't turn him down.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jones Hank — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Roy Haynes
about_quotes.evidence: Maybe sometime in between I would do something with somebody else, with Babs Gonzales or somebody
about_quotes.doc_title: Roy Haynes — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
